watering.description: Allow the top inch of soil to dry out between waterings. Drooping leaves are a reliable signal it's time to water.
underwatering_signs: soil pulling away from pot edges

light.description: Highly adaptable. Thrives in bright indirect light but tolerates low light. Direct sun will scorch the leaves.

pothos.fertilizing: monthly during spring and summer with a balanced liquid fertilizer; stop in fall and winter
common_issues: yellowing leaves from overwatering
leggy and sparse growth in low light
brown tips from inconsistent watering or dry air

seasonal_notes.spring: Resume fertilizing. Increase watering frequency as growth picks up.
seasonal_notes.summer: Water more frequently. Watch for fungus gnats if soil stays too wet.
seasonal_notes.fall: Taper off fertilizer. Start reducing watering frequency.
seasonal_notes.winter: Water sparingly. Bright indirect light becomes more important. No fertilizer.

watering.description: One of the most drought-tolerant houseplants. Allow soil to dry completely between waterings. When in doubt, wait.

light.description: Survives almost anywhere. Grows fastest in bright indirect light but is genuinely one of the best low-light plants.

snake_plant.humidity: low humidity tolerant — thrives in typical dry indoor environments
snake_plant.fertilizing: 2–3 times per year during the growing season; never in winter
common_issues: root rot from overwatering — the most common cause of death
brown tips from fluoride in tap water or physical damage
slow growth in low light (normal, not a problem)

seasonal_notes.spring: Repot if root-bound. Begin light fertilizing.
seasonal_notes.summer: Water slightly more often. Check for spider mites in hot dry conditions.
seasonal_notes.winter: Water once a month or less. Keep away from cold drafts.

watering.description: The rhizomes store water, making it very forgiving. Let soil dry completely before watering.
underwatering_signs: leaf yellowing starting from the bottom

light.description: One of the best low-light plants. Grows slowly but steadily even in dim rooms. Avoid direct sunlight.
light.avoid: direct sunlight — will scorch and bleach leaves

zz_plant.humidity: tolerant of low humidity; no special requirements
zz_plant.fertilizing: monthly in spring and summer with diluted balanced fertilizer
common_issues: yellowing from overwatering
very slow growth (normal — be patient)
all parts are toxic if ingested — keep away from pets and children

seasonal_notes.spring: Begin fertilizing. May push out new growth.
seasonal_notes.summer: Water slightly more often. Can handle heat well.
seasonal_notes.winter: Minimal water needed. Growth will slow or stop entirely.

watering.description: Keep soil evenly moist during the growing season, slightly drier in winter. Sensitive to fluoride — use filtered or distilled water if possible.
underwatering_signs: dry soil pulling from pot edges

light.description: Thrives in bright indirect light. Tolerates moderate indirect light but variegation may fade. Avoid direct sun.
light.avoid: direct sunlight — scorches leaves and fades variegation

spider_plant.humidity: average household humidity; benefits from occasional misting
spider_plant.fertilizing: monthly during growing season with balanced fertilizer
common_issues: brown leaf tips from fluoride in tap water or dry air
fading variegation in low light
spiderettes (babies) growing from long stems — these can be propagated easily

seasonal_notes.spring: Resume fertilizing. Great time to propagate spiderettes.
seasonal_notes.summer: Water more frequently. May produce more spiderettes.
seasonal_notes.winter: Water less frequently. Avoid cold windowsills.

watering.description: Likes consistently moist (not wet) soil. Peace lilies will dramatically droop when thirsty — and bounce back quickly once watered. This drooping is a helpful signal, not permanent damage.

light.description: One of the few flowering plants that does well in low light. Flowers best with medium indirect light. Avoid direct sun.
light.avoid: direct sunlight — will scorch and yellow leaves

peace_lily.humidity: prefers high humidity (50%+); mist regularly or use a pebble tray
peace_lily.fertilizing: monthly during spring and summer; withhold in fall and winter
common_issues: browning tips from dry air or inconsistent watering
yellowing leaves from overwatering
failure to bloom in low light conditions
toxic to pets — keep away from cats and dogs

seasonal_notes.spring: Most likely to bloom in spring. Resume fertilizing.
seasonal_notes.summer: Increase watering. Watch for spider mites in hot, dry conditions.
seasonal_notes.fall: Reduce fertilizing. Begin reducing watering frequency.
seasonal_notes.winter: Keep away from cold windows. Water sparingly.

watering.frequency: every 2–3 weeks in summer, monthly in winter
watering.description: Water deeply and infrequently. Allow soil to dry completely between waterings. Aloe stores water in its leaves and will rot quickly in soggy soil.

light.requirement: bright indirect to some direct sun
light.description: Loves lots of light. Does well in a south or west-facing window. Can handle a few hours of direct morning sun.
light.avoid: intense afternoon direct sun in summer — can cause sunburn

aloe_vera.humidity: tolerates low humidity; prefers dry conditions
aloe_vera.fertilizing: once in spring with diluted cactus fertilizer; rarely needs fertilizing
common_issues: root rot from overwatering — the primary cause of death
sunburn from sudden exposure to intense direct sun
pups (offsets) crowding the pot — divide when this happens
leggy growth reaching for light

seasonal_notes.spring: Resume watering. Fertilize once. Repot if needed.
seasonal_notes.summer: Water more but ensure soil dries fully. Protect from intense afternoon sun.
seasonal_notes.winter: Water monthly or less. Keep above 50°F. No fertilizer.

watering.description: Water thoroughly when the top 2 inches of soil feel dry. Monsteras like to dry out slightly between waterings but shouldn't stay bone dry.

light.description: Thrives in bright indirect light. Leaf fenestration (the iconic holes) only develops well with sufficient light. Can tolerate medium indirect light but growth and splitting will be reduced.

monstera.humidity: prefers high humidity (50%+); mist, use a pebble tray, or group with other plants
monstera.fertilizing: monthly during spring and summer with balanced fertilizer; reduce in fall and stop in winter
common_issues: yellowing leaves from overwatering or nutrient deficiency
leaves without holes from insufficient light
root rot in heavy or consistently wet soil
aerial roots — normal, can be tucked into soil or a moss pole

seasonal_notes.spring: Most active growth period. Fertilize monthly. Good time to repot.
seasonal_notes.summer: Water more frequently. May need staking as it grows. Watch for spider mites.
seasonal_notes.winter: Minimal water. No fertilizer. Keep away from cold drafts.

watering.description: Water when the top inch of soil feels dry. Rubber plants hate inconsistency — try to water on a regular schedule. Wipe leaves with a damp cloth occasionally to remove dust.

light.description: Does best with several hours of bright indirect light daily. Darker-leaved varieties tolerate lower light better than lighter ones.
light.avoid: direct sun — can bleach the leaves; sudden changes in light cause leaf drop

rubber_plant.humidity: average household humidity is fine; prefers 40–60%
rubber_plant.temperature: 60–80°F (15–27°C); avoid cold drafts and temperatures below 55°F
rubber_plant.fertilizing: monthly during growing season; no fertilizer in winter
common_issues: dramatic leaf drop from temperature changes, drafts, or repotting stress
yellowing lower leaves from overwatering
leggy growth in insufficient light
sap from cuts can irritate skin — wear gloves when pruning

seasonal_notes.summer: Water regularly. Good time to prune for shape.
seasonal_notes.winter: Water sparingly. Keep away from cold windows. No fertilizer.

watering.frequency: every few days — keep soil consistently moist
watering.description: Boston ferns want consistently moist soil — not wet, not dry. This is the most demanding plant in the database for watering consistency. They will brown quickly if allowed to dry out.

light.description: Prefers bright indirect light. Avoid direct sun. Does reasonably well in moderate indirect light.

boston_fern.humidity: requires high humidity (50%+); mist daily, use a humidifier, or place on a pebble tray with water
boston_fern.fertilizing: monthly during growing season with diluted balanced fertilizer
common_issues: browning fronds from low humidity — the most common issue
frond drop when moved or disturbed
spider mites and scale in dry conditions
shed fronds make a mess — consider placement carefully

seasonal_notes.spring: Resume regular fertilizing. Growth picks up significantly.
seasonal_notes.summer: Mist daily in hot, dry weather. Water frequently. Watch for pests.
seasonal_notes.winter: Most challenging season. Dry indoor air causes browning. Use a humidifier.

watering.description: Water by soaking the roots (or pot) thoroughly, then let drain and dry almost completely before watering again. Roots should be silver-gray when dry, green when wet. Many orchids come in clear pots specifically so you can check root moisture.
overwatering_signs: limp leaves that don't firm up when watered
underwatering_signs: silver-gray roots for more than 2 weeks

light.description: Needs bright indirect light to rebloom. An east-facing window is ideal. Can receive gentle morning sun.
light.avoid: direct afternoon sun — will bleach and burn leaves

orchid.humidity: prefers 50–70% humidity; mist roots (not flowers) or use a humidity tray
orchid.temperature: 65–80°F (18–27°C); a 10°F temperature drop at night helps trigger reblooming
orchid.fertilizing: weakly weekly during active growth ('weekly, weakly') with orchid-specific fertilizer; reduce in winter
common_issues: failure to rebloom — usually insufficient light or no night temperature drop
root rot from keeping roots wet too long
yellow leaves from too much direct light
crown rot if water pools at the center of the plant

seasonal_notes.spring: After blooming, cut spike to a node below lowest bloom. New spike may emerge.
seasonal_notes.fall: Cooler nights help trigger new spike development.
seasonal_notes.winter: Reduce fertilizing. Cool nights (55–60°F) encourage reblooming.

watering.description: Water when the top 2 inches of soil are dry. Consistency is everything — same schedule, same amount, same location. Fiddle leaf figs react badly to change.
underwatering_signs: crunchy brown spots without yellow halos

light.description: Needs several hours of bright indirect light daily. A consistent spot near a window is critical — moving this plant frequently causes dramatic leaf drop.
light.avoid: direct afternoon sun; frequent relocation; cold drafts

fiddle_leaf_fig.humidity: moderate humidity (40–60%); avoid dry conditions near heaters
fiddle_leaf_fig.temperature: 60–75°F (15–24°C); highly sensitive to cold drafts and temperature fluctuations
fiddle_leaf_fig.fertilizing: monthly during growing season with balanced fertilizer; never in winter
common_issues: brown spots from overwatering (wet spots) or underwatering (dry spots) — the cause matters for the fix
leaf drop from drafts, relocation, or inconsistent watering
slow recovery from stress — be patient after changes
root rot if planted in heavy soil or kept too wet

seasonal_notes.spring: Growth period begins. Fertilize monthly. Repot if root-bound.
seasonal_notes.summer: Water consistently. Can be moved outdoors in warm climates (acclimate slowly).
seasonal_notes.fall: Reduce watering and fertilizing. Avoid moving the plant.
seasonal_notes.winter: Minimal water. No fertilizer. Keep far from cold windows and heating vents.

watering.description: Keep soil consistently moist but not soggy. Calatheas are sensitive to minerals in tap water — use filtered, distilled, or rainwater to prevent brown edges. Let water sit overnight if using tap.
underwatering_signs: leaves remaining curled in the morning

light.description: Prefers indirect or filtered light. Direct sun will bleach and damage the distinctive leaf markings. Tolerates lower light better than most.
light.avoid: direct sunlight — destroys the decorative leaf patterns

calathea.humidity: requires high humidity (50%+); use a humidifier, mist frequently, or group with other plants
calathea.temperature: 60–80°F (15–27°C); sensitive to cold drafts and temperatures below 55°F
calathea.fertilizing: monthly during growing season with diluted balanced fertilizer
common_issues: brown leaf edges from tap water minerals or dry air — the most common issue
leaf curling from underwatering or low humidity
yellowing from overwatering
generally fussy — it's normal for this plant to require adjustment time in a new home

seasonal_notes.spring: Growth resumes. Use filtered water. Fertilize monthly.
seasonal_notes.summer: Increase humidity. Water consistently. Keep out of direct sun.
seasonal_notes.fall: Reduce fertilizer. Maintain humidity — this is when indoor heating starts drying the air.
seasonal_notes.winter: Hardest season for calatheas. Indoor heating dries air significantly. Use a humidifier.

watering.description: Water when the top inch of soil feels dry. Similar care to pothos — both are forgiving and communicate their needs through drooping.

light.description: Extremely adaptable. Grows fastest in bright indirect light but tolerates lower light conditions well. Trailing varieties work great in hanging baskets.

philodendron.humidity: average to high humidity; benefits from occasional misting
common_issues: leggy growth with large leaf spacing in low light
yellowing from overwatering
mealybugs and spider mites in dry conditions
toxic to pets — keep out of reach

seasonal_notes.spring: Active growth period. Resume fertilizing. Good time to propagate.
seasonal_notes.summer: Water more frequently. Can grow quickly — trim to encourage bushiness.
seasonal_notes.winter: Water sparingly. Provide as much light as possible.

watering.description: Allow the top inch to dry between waterings. More tolerant than most of both under and overwatering, making it very beginner-friendly.

light.description: One of the best low-light plants. Darker-leaved varieties tolerate lower light; lighter variegated varieties prefer more light. Avoid direct sun regardless of variety.
light.avoid: direct sunlight — will bleach and scorch leaves

chinese_evergreen.humidity: average household humidity (40–60%); tolerant of dry conditions
chinese_evergreen.fertilizing: twice a year during growing season; light feeder
common_issues: yellowing lower leaves from overwatering
brown tips from dry air or fluoride in tap water
all parts are toxic to pets

seasonal_notes.spring: Fertilize once. May push new growth.
seasonal_notes.summer: Water slightly more. Watch for pests.
seasonal_notes.winter: Minimal water. Keep away from drafts.

watering.frequency: every 2–3 weeks in growing season, monthly or less in winter
watering.description: Water thoroughly, then allow soil to dry out completely before watering again. 'Soak and dry' method. The number one killer of succulents is too much water.
overwatering_signs: leaves falling off with a touch
black or brown mushy stem base
underwatering_signs: leaves feel less plump than usual

light.description: Most succulents want as much light as possible — a south or west-facing window is ideal. Many can handle direct sun once acclimated.
light.avoid: low light — will cause etiolation (stretching toward the light) and weak, pale growth

succulent.humidity: prefers low humidity; poorly suited to humid climates or bathrooms
succulent.temperature: 60–80°F (15–27°C) for most varieties; some are cold-hardy outdoors
succulent.fertilizing: once in early spring with diluted cactus fertilizer; succulents are very light feeders
common_issues: etiolation (stretching, leaning, weak growth) from insufficient light
root rot from overwatering or poor drainage
mealybugs hiding in the rosette center
sunburn from sudden exposure to intense direct sun without acclimation

seasonal_notes.spring: Resume watering. Fertilize once. Best time to repot.
seasonal_notes.summer: Most active growth period. Water when fully dry. Protect from intense afternoon sun.
seasonal_notes.winter: Water once a month or less. Dormancy period for many varieties.
